reaction_name: 1,3-Dipolar cycloaddition of in-situ-generated nitrile oxide with phenylacetylene to form ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate
bond_formed: C-O and C-C bonds forming the isoxazole ring
bond_broken: N-O bond rearrangement in ethyl nitroacetate during nitrile oxide generation; loss of H2O

reagents: phenylacetylene (1.0 equiv, 0.5 M in TBAB/EG 1:5 DES)
feed_streams.reasoning: Dipolarophile dissolved in DES; separate stream to control stoichiometry and mixing

reagents: ethyl nitroacetate (2.0 equiv, 1.0 M in TBAB/EG 1:5 DES)
feed_streams.reasoning: Nitrile oxide precursor in DES; kept separate until mixing point to avoid premature nitrile oxide dimerization at elevated temperature before proper mixing

reagents.name: ethyl nitroacetate
reagents.notes: Generates ethoxycarbonyl nitrile oxide in situ via thermal dehydration; 2 equiv used to drive reaction to completion

reagents.name: TBAB (tetrabutylammonium bromide)
reagents.role: DES component (hydrogen bond acceptor) / reaction medium / promoter
reagents.equiv_or_loading: 1:5 molar ratio with ethylene glycol (1 mL total DES)
reagents.notes: Forms deep eutectic solvent with ethylene glycol; acts as both solvent and promoter for nitrile oxide generation

reagents.name: ethylene glycol
reagents.role: DES component (hydrogen bond donor) / reaction medium
reagents.notes: Hydrogen bond donor component of the DES

mechanism_steps.description: Thermal dehydration of ethyl nitroacetate to generate ethoxycarbonyl nitrile oxide (EtOOC-CNO) in situ. The DES promotes this tautomerization/elimination at 120°C.

Concerted [3+2] 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition of the transient nitrile oxide (1,3-dipole) with phenylacetylene (dipolarophile) to form the isoxazole ring. Regioselectivity favors 3,5-disubstituted isoxazole (phenyl at C5, ester at C3).
Aromatization/tautomerization of the initial cycloadduct to give the fully aromatic ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate product.

stream_logic.reasoning: Dipolarophile in DES; kept separate to ensure controlled mixing with nitrile oxide precursor and proper stoichiometric ratio via flow rate control

Nitrile oxide precursor in DES; 2.0 equiv relative to phenylacetylene achieved by concentration/flow rate ratio. Separate feed prevents premature nitrile oxide generation and dimerization before efficient mixing

mixing_order_reasoning: Both streams should be mixed rapidly at a T-mixer or static mixer immediately before entering the heated coil reactor. The DES is viscous at room temperature (~50-100 cP), so both feed lines should be preheated to ~60-80°C to reduce viscosity and ensure good mixing. Rapid mixing is critical because the nitrile oxide intermediate is transient and prone to dimerization (furoxan byproduct) if local concentration is too high. The 2:1 stoichiometry (ethyl nitroacetate : phenylacetylene) is achieved by setting Stream B concentration or flow rate to deliver 2.0 equiv.
deoxygenation_reasoning: No oxygen-sensitive species or radicals involved; thermal pericyclic mechanism is unaffected by O2.
quench_reasoning: The batch protocol quenches with water for workup/extraction purposes only, not to stop a dangerous reaction. In flow, the product stream can be collected directly and worked up offline.
wavelength_reasoning: This is a purely thermal reaction; no photocatalyst or photochemical activation is involved.
confidence_notes: High confidence in mechanism (well-established nitrile oxide [3+2] cycloaddition). Moderate uncertainty about DES viscosity handling in flow — TBAB/EG DES is quite viscous at room temperature and may require preheating of feed lines to 60-80°C for reliable pumping and mixing. The exact mechanism of nitrile oxide generation from ethyl nitroacetate in DES (whether purely thermal dehydration or DES-assisted) is not fully characterized in literature. Pressure may need to be elevated (back-pressure regulator) to keep ethylene glycol/DES stable at 120°C and prevent any volatile loss. The 2.0 equiv of ethyl nitroacetate suggests nitrile oxide dimerization is a competing pathway, so excess is used to compensate.
